title: Lorenz Hart

infobox.value: Lorenz Milton Hart  (1895-05-02)May 2, 1895  [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), U.S.

(May 2, 1895 – November 22, 1943) was an American lyricist and half of the [Broadway](/wiki/Broadway_theatre "Broadway theatre") songwriting team [Rodgers and Hart](/wiki/Rodgers_and_Hart "Rodgers and Hart"). Some of his more famous lyrics include "[Blue Moon](/wiki/Blue_Moon_(1934_song) "Blue Moon (1934 song)")"; "[The Lady Is a Tramp](/wiki/The_Lady_Is_a_Tramp "The Lady Is a Tramp")"; "[Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan_(song) "Manhattan (song)")"; "[Bewitched, Bothered and Bewildered](/wiki/Bewitched,_Bothered_and_Bewildered "Bewitched, Bothered and Bewildered")"; and "[My Funny Valentine](/wiki/My_Funny_Valentine "My Funny Valentine")".

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

American lyricist (1895–1943)

| Lorenz Hart | |
| --- | --- |
| [Hart in 1936](/wiki/File:Lorenz_Hart_NYWTS_(cropped).jpg "Hart in 1936") Hart in 1936 | |
| Background information | |
| Born | Lorenz Milton Hart  (1895-05-02)May 2, 1895  [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), U.S. |
| Died | November 22, 1943(1943-11-22) (aged 48)  New York City, U.S. |
| Genres | Musical theatre |
| Occupation | Lyricist |
| Years active | 1919–1943 |

Musical artist

**Lorenz Milton Hart** (May 2, 1895 – November 22, 1943) was an American lyricist and half of the [Broadway](/wiki/Broadway_theatre "Broadway theatre") songwriting team [Rodgers and Hart](/wiki/Rodgers_and_Hart "Rodgers and Hart"). Some of his more famous lyrics include "[Blue Moon](/wiki/Blue_Moon_(1934_song) "Blue Moon (1934 song)")"; "[The Lady Is a Tramp](/wiki/The_Lady_Is_a_Tramp "The Lady Is a Tramp")"; "[Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan_(song) "Manhattan (song)")"; "[Bewitched, Bothered and Bewildered](/wiki/Bewitched,_Bothered_and_Bewildered "Bewitched, Bothered and Bewildered")"; and "[My Funny Valentine](/wiki/My_Funny_Valentine "My Funny Valentine")".

## Life and career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lorenz_Hart&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Life and career")]

Hart was born in [Harlem](/wiki/Harlem "Harlem"), New York City, the elder of two sons, to German Jewish immigrant parents, Max M. and Frieda ([née](/wiki/N%C3%A9e "Née") Isenberg) Hart. Through his mother, he was a great-grandnephew of the German poet [Heinrich Heine](/wiki/Heinrich_Heine "Heinrich Heine").[[1]](#cite_note-1) His father, a business promoter, sent Hart and his brother to private schools. (His brother, Teddy Hart, also went into theatre and became a musical comedy star. Teddy Hart's wife, Dorothy Hart, wrote a biography of Lorenz Hart.)[[2]](#cite_note-Mooney-2)

Hart's exposure to theater began through his parents, who took him to Yiddish and German theater productions in New York. When they were old enough, Hart and his brother attended the theater on their own. Precociously well read, he published poems and fiction in student publications and at summer camp wrote comedic revues for fellow campers.[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarmorstein201223–35-3)

Hart received his early education at [Columbia Grammar School](/wiki/Columbia_Grammar_School "Columbia Grammar School") and entered [Columbia College](/wiki/Columbia_College_(New_York) "Columbia College (New York)") in 1913, graduating in 1918.[[4]](#cite_note-4) From 1914 to 1916, he also spent two years at [Columbia University School of Journalism](/wiki/Columbia_University_School_of_Journalism "Columbia University School of Journalism").[[2]](#cite_note-Mooney-2)[[5]](#cite_note-Biography-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6) While he had no special interest in becoming a journalist, he was drawn to the school's steady requirement of writing. While at Columbia, he also took a class in dramatic technique.[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarmorstein201234-7)

By 1918, Hart was working for the [Shubert](/wiki/Shubert_family "Shubert family") brothers, partners in theatre, translating German play songs into English. In 1919, a friend introduced him to [Richard Rodgers](/wiki/Richard_Rodgers "Richard Rodgers"), and the two joined forces to write songs for a series of amateur and student productions.[[2]](#cite_note-Mooney-2) That year, his and Rodgers's song "Any Old Place With You" was included in the Broadway musical comedy *A Lonely Romeo*. In 1920, six of their songs were used in the musical comedy *[Poor Little Ritz Girl](/wiki/Poor_Little_Ritz_Girl "Poor Little Ritz Girl")*, which also had music by [Sigmund Romberg](/wiki/Sigmund_Romberg "Sigmund Romberg"). They were hired to write the score for the 1925 [Theatre Guild](/wiki/Theatre_Guild "Theatre Guild") production *[The Garrick Gaieties](/wiki/The_Garrick_Gaieties "The Garrick Gaieties")*, the success of which brought them acclaim.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Rodgers and Hart subsequently wrote the music and lyrics for 26 Broadway musicals during a partnership of more than 20 years that ended shortly before Hart's death. Their "big four" were *[Babes in Arms](/wiki/Babes_in_Arms "Babes in Arms"),* *[The Boys from Syracuse](/wiki/The_Boys_from_Syracuse "The Boys from Syracuse")*, *[Pal Joey](/wiki/Pal_Joey_(musical) "Pal Joey (musical)")*, and *[On Your Toes](/wiki/On_Your_Toes "On Your Toes").* The Rodgers and Hart songs have been described as intimate and destined for long lives outside the theater.[[8]](#cite_note-HoldenPop-8) Many of them are standard repertoire for singers and jazz musicians. Hart has been called "the expressive bard of the urban generation which matured during the interwar years".[[2]](#cite_note-Mooney-2) But the "encomiums suggest[ing] that Larry Hart was a poet"[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarmorstein201214-9) caused his friend and fellow writer Henry Myers to say otherwise: "Larry in particular was primarily a showman. If you can manage to examine his songs technically, and for the moment elude their spell, you will see that they are all meant to be acted, that they are part of a play. Larry was a playwright."[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarmorstein201214-9)

Rodgers and Hart wrote music and lyrics for several films, including *[Love Me Tonight](/wiki/Love_Me_Tonight "Love Me Tonight")* (1932), *[The Phantom President](/wiki/The_Phantom_President "The Phantom President")* (1932), *[Hallelujah, I'm a Bum](/wiki/Hallelujah,_I%27m_a_Bum_(film) "Hallelujah, I'm a Bum (film)")* (1933), and *[Mississippi](/wiki/Mississippi_(film) "Mississippi (film)")* (1935).[[5]](#cite_note-Biography-5) With their successes, during the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression") Hart was earning $60,000 annually, and he became a magnet for many people. He gave numerous large parties. Beginning in 1938, he traveled more often and suffered from his ongoing [drinking](/wiki/Alcoholism "Alcoholism").[[10]](#cite_note-Nolan-10) Nevertheless, Rodgers and Hart continued working together through mid-1942, with their final new musical being 1942's *[By Jupiter](/wiki/By_Jupiter "By Jupiter")*.

*[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* reported on July 23, 1942: "The [Theatre Guild](/wiki/Theatre_Guild "Theatre Guild") announced yesterday that [Richard Rodgers](/wiki/Richard_Rodgers "Richard Rodgers"), Lorenz Hart and [Oscar Hammerstein II](/wiki/Oscar_Hammerstein_II "Oscar Hammerstein II") will soon begin work on a musical version of [Lynn Riggs](/wiki/Lynn_Riggs "Lynn Riggs")'s folk-play, *[Green Grow the Lilacs](/wiki/Green_Grow_the_Lilacs_(play) "Green Grow the Lilacs (play)")*, which the Guild produced for sixty-four performances at the [Guild Theatre](/wiki/Guild_Theatre_(Portland,_Oregon) "Guild Theatre (Portland, Oregon)") in 1931." Rodgers had brought Hammerstein onto the project due to Hart's skepticism that the play could be adapted into a musical. Hart said he had difficulty writing a musical for such a rural setting as Oklahoma.[[11]](#cite_note-11) He was not alone in this assessment. [Jerome Kern](/wiki/Jerome_Kern "Jerome Kern") had also been offered the project and was equally doubtful that the play, which had run only 64 performances, would succeed as a musical. Disappointed at Hart's refusal, Rodgers turned to Hammerstein.[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENolan1995298–299-12) Complicating this was Hart's worsening mental state and exhaustion after his work on *[By Jupiter](/wiki/By_Jupiter "By Jupiter")*.[[13]](#cite_note-13) This left an eager Hammerstein to complete what would became *[Oklahoma!](/wiki/Oklahoma! "Oklahoma!").*[[14]](#cite_note-14) [Rodgers and Hammerstein](/wiki/Rodgers_and_Hammerstein "Rodgers and Hammerstein") continued to collaborate for 16 years (until Hammerstein died, in 1960), a partnership that made them one of the most successful composing teams of the 20th century.

After the success of *Oklahoma!* in 1943, Rodgers decided that he and Hart should reunite for a revival of their successful 1927 musical [*A Connecticut Yankee*](/wiki/A_Connecticut_Yankee_(musical) "A Connecticut Yankee (musical)"). Hart composed new lyrics for many of the songs in anticipation of the revival's November 17, 1943 premiere at the [Martin Beck Theatre](/wiki/Al_Hirschfeld_Theatre "Al Hirschfeld Theatre").[[15]](#cite_note-Gottlieb-15) Six new numbers, including "[To Keep My Love Alive](/wiki/To_Keep_My_Love_Alive "To Keep My Love Alive")", were written for this reworked version of the play; it was Hart's last lyric.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Lyrical style

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lorenz_Hart&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Lyrical style")]

According to Thomas Hischak, Hart "had a remarkable talent for polysyllabic and [internal rhymes](/wiki/Internal_rhyme "Internal rhyme")",[[16]](#cite_note-16) and his lyrics have often been praised for their wit and technical sophistication.

Writer, composer, and critic [Anthony Burgess](/wiki/Anthony_Burgess "Anthony Burgess") singled out Hart as among the first to use polysyllabic rhyme not to be humorous but as a source of wit and insight: "wit in the service of frustration or neurosis."[[17]](#cite_note-Burgess,_1983-17) In Burgess's view, Hart was also among the early practitioners of a more colloquial, conversational style of lyric writing for musical theater: "The effect is of a very conversational declaration, which by the happiest of accidents, has rhymes in it."[[17]](#cite_note-Burgess,_1983-17)

According to music critic [Stephen Holden](/wiki/Stephen_Holden "Stephen Holden"), "Many of Hart's ballad lyrics conveyed a heart-stopping sadness that reflected his conviction that he was physically too unattractive to be lovable."[[18]](#cite_note-18) Holden also wrote, "In his lyrics, as in his life, Hart stands as a compellingly lonely figure. Although he wrote dozens of songs that are playful, funny and filled with clever wordplay, it is the rueful vulnerability beneath their surface that lends them a singular poignancy."[[8]](#cite_note-HoldenPop-8)

## Personal life and death

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lorenz_Hart&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Personal life and death")]

Screenwriter [Robert Kaplow](/wiki/Robert_Kaplow "Robert Kaplow") said Hart was "so uncomfortable in his own skin that the way he probably got through life was he knew he had to be the funniest, smartest guy in the room".[[19]](#cite_note-Zuckerman-19) His alcoholism sometimes led to weeks-long disappearances on drinking binges.[[2]](#cite_note-Mooney-2) Until her death in April 1943, Hart lived with his widowed mother, Frieda; devastated by her loss, he sank deeper into melancholy and died seven months later.[[20]](#cite_note-NolanP2-20)

Hart's erratic behavior was often the cause of friction between him and Rodgers and led Rodgers to team with lyricist [Oscar Hammerstein II](/wiki/Oscar_Hammerstein_II "Oscar Hammerstein II") in 1942. On March 31, 1943, Hart attended the Broadway premiere of Rodgers and Hammerstein's *Oklahoma!*. Hart briefly appeared at the opening night party at [Sardi's](/wiki/Sardi%27s "Sardi's") and told Rodgers, "This is one of the greatest shows I've ever seen, and it'll be playing twenty years from now!" He left with his mother immediately afterward, saying she was tired.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarmorstein2012440-21)[[15]](#cite_note-Gottlieb-15)

Many of Hart's contemporaries who knew him socially said he was a discreet homosexual, with a reputation as a [voyeur](/wiki/Voyeur "Voyeur").[[15]](#cite_note-Gottlieb-15)

[Robert Gottlieb](/wiki/Robert_Gottlieb "Robert Gottlieb") wrote in the April 2013 edition of *[The Atlantic](/wiki/The_Atlantic "The Atlantic")* magazine:

> There were rumors about Larry [Lorenz Hart] while he was alive, but nothing about his sexuality ever appeared in print. One night in Los Angeles, in 1933, someone from a Hollywood trade magazine approached Dick [Richard Rodgers] at a party and said, "I've got to ask you something about Larry ... Is it true Larry's a fairy?" Dick grabbed him by the collar, [biographer Gary] Marmorstein recounts, and said, "I never heard that. And if you print it, I'll kill you."[[15]](#cite_note-Gottlieb-15)

On the opening night of *A Connecticut Yankee*, Hart showed up drunk.[[15]](#cite_note-Gottlieb-15) His condition was noticed by his sister-in-law, and she persuaded him to accompany her to her Manhattan home.[[15]](#cite_note-Gottlieb-15) Sometime after they arrived, Hart left, venturing into cold weather to resume drinking.[[15]](#cite_note-Gottlieb-15) A friend of Hart's found him sitting on the curb in front of a bar he favored on Manhattan's Eighth Avenue.[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENolan1995310–311-22) Hart was shivering, and the friend accompanied him to a hospital,[[15]](#cite_note-Gottlieb-15) where it was determined that Hart had developed [pneumonia](/wiki/Pneumonia "Pneumonia") from exposure. On November 22, 1943, about four days after admission to the hospital, he died.[[20]](#cite_note-NolanP2-20)[[15]](#cite_note-Gottlieb-15)

Hart is buried in [Mount Zion Cemetery](/wiki/Mount_Zion_Cemetery_(New_York_City) "Mount Zion Cemetery (New York City)") in [Queens](/wiki/Queens "Queens") County, New York.[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24)

## In popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lorenz_Hart&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: In popular culture")]

The circumstances of Hart's life were heavily altered and romanticized in the 1948 [MGM](/wiki/MGM "MGM") biopic *[Words and Music](/wiki/Words_and_Music_(1948_film) "Words and Music (1948 film)")*, with fictitious personal details such as changing his sexual orientation and attributing his erratic behavior and depression to an obsession with a woman, played by [Betty Garrett](/wiki/Betty_Garrett "Betty Garrett"), who turns down his marriage proposal.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

In 2025, the film *[Blue Moon](/wiki/Blue_Moon_(2025_film) "Blue Moon (2025 film)")* was released. It is set during Hart's last days, mostly around [Sardi's Restaurant](/wiki/Sardi%27s_Restaurant "Sardi's Restaurant") on March 31, 1943, on the opening night of *[Oklahoma!](/wiki/Oklahoma! "Oklahoma!")*. Written by [Robert Kaplow](/wiki/Robert_Kaplow "Robert Kaplow"), the film is directed by [Richard Linklater](/wiki/Richard_Linklater "Richard Linklater") and stars [Ethan Hawke](/wiki/Ethan_Hawke "Ethan Hawke") as Hart, alongside [Margaret Qualley](/wiki/Margaret_Qualley "Margaret Qualley"), [Bobby Cannavale](/wiki/Bobby_Cannavale "Bobby Cannavale"), and [Andrew Scott](/wiki/Andrew_Scott_(actor) "Andrew Scott (actor)") as Rodgers.[[26]](#cite_note-26) The film had its world premiere at the main competition of the [75th Berlin International Film Festival](/wiki/75th_Berlin_International_Film_Festival "75th Berlin International Film Festival"), on February 18, 2025, where it won the [Silver Bear for Best Supporting Performance](/wiki/Silver_Bear_for_Best_Supporting_Performance "Silver Bear for Best Supporting Performance") for Scott. It received two nominations for [Best Actor in a Motion Picture – Comedy or Musical](/wiki/Best_Actor_in_a_Motion_Picture_%E2%80%93_Comedy_or_Musical "Best Actor in a Motion Picture – Comedy or Musical") for Hawke and [Best Motion Picture – Musical or Comedy](/wiki/Best_Motion_Picture_%E2%80%93_Musical_or_Comedy "Best Motion Picture – Musical or Comedy") at the [83rd Golden Globe Awards](/wiki/83rd_Golden_Globe_Awards "83rd Golden Globe Awards").[[27]](#cite_note-27) *Blue Moon* received a widespread release on October 24, 2025.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

## Selected stage works

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lorenz_Hart&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Selected stage works")]

* 1920 [*Fly with Me*](/wiki/Fly_with_Me_(musical) "Fly with Me (musical)")
* 1920 *[Poor Little Ritz Girl](/wiki/Poor_Little_Ritz_Girl "Poor Little Ritz Girl")*
* 1925 *[The Garrick Gaieties](/wiki/The_Garrick_Gaieties "The Garrick Gaieties")*
* 1925 *[Dearest Enemy](/wiki/Dearest_Enemy "Dearest Enemy")*
* 1926 *[The Girl Friend](/wiki/The_Girl_Friend "The Girl Friend")*
* 1926 *[Peggy-Ann](/wiki/Peggy-Ann "Peggy-Ann")*
* 1926 *[The Garrick Gaieties](/wiki/The_Garrick_Gaieties "The Garrick Gaieties")* (2nd Edition)
* 1927 *[A Connecticut Yankee](/wiki/A_Connecticut_Yankee_(musical) "A Connecticut Yankee (musical)")*, based on the [Mark Twain](/wiki/Mark_Twain "Mark Twain") novel *[A Connecticut Yankee in King Arthur's Court](/wiki/A_Connecticut_Yankee_in_King_Arthur%27s_Court "A Connecticut Yankee in King Arthur's Court")*
* 1928 *[Present Arms](/wiki/Present_Arms_(musical) "Present Arms (musical)")*
* 1930 *[Simple Simon](/wiki/Simple_Simon_(musical) "Simple Simon (musical)")*
* 1935 *[Jumbo](/wiki/Jumbo_(musical) "Jumbo (musical)")*
* 1936 *[On Your Toes](/wiki/On_Your_Toes "On Your Toes")*
* 1937 *[Babes in Arms](/wiki/Babes_in_Arms "Babes in Arms")*
* 1937 *[I'd Rather Be Right](/wiki/I%27d_Rather_Be_Right "I'd Rather Be Right")*
* 1938 *[The Boys from Syracuse](/wiki/The_Boys_from_Syracuse "The Boys from Syracuse")*, based on [William Shakespeare](/wiki/William_Shakespeare "William Shakespeare")'s *[The Comedy of Errors](/wiki/The_Comedy_of_Errors "The Comedy of Errors")*
* 1938 *[I Married an Angel](/wiki/I_Married_an_Angel "I Married an Angel")*
* 1939 *[Too Many Girls](/wiki/Too_Many_Girls_(musical) "Too Many Girls (musical)")*
* 1940 *[Higher and Higher](/wiki/Higher_and_Higher_(musical) "Higher and Higher (musical)")*
* 1940 *[Pal Joey](/wiki/Pal_Joey_(musical) "Pal Joey (musical)")*, based on [John O'Hara](/wiki/John_O%27Hara "John O'Hara")'s novel *[Pal Joey](/wiki/Pal_Joey_(novel) "Pal Joey (novel)")*
* 1942 *[By Jupiter](/wiki/By_Jupiter "By Jupiter")*
